summary: Gruber draws a direct parallel between Reddit's shutdown of third-party API access and Twitter's earlier killing of third-party clients, noting that Apollo developer Christian Selig now faces the same financial cliff as Tweetbot and Twitterrific did. The core problem is identical: developers who collected annual subscriptions are suddenly on the hook for prorated refunds when the platform pulls access without warning. Gruber encourages Apollo subscribers to tap the 'I Don't Need a Refund' button to support Selig. He extensively quotes Selig's gracious farewell post, in which the developer expresses heartbreak at Reddit's bad faith but gratitude for nine years of building Apollo alongside its community. Gruber finds Selig's positive attitude in the face of the debacle to be inspiring.
key_points: The Apollo shutdown mirrors the Tweetbot/Twitterrific situation exactly: platform kills third-party access, developer owes hundreds of thousands in subscription refunds
This pattern of platforms destroying third-party client ecosystems without warning or sunset periods is becoming a recurring theme in tech
Christian Selig believed Reddit was acting in good faith but concluded they were dishonest, callous, and punitive in their API pricing
Gruber highlights the specific financial double-punch: developers don't just lose future income, they owe back money already collected from subscribers
money_quotes: Now imagine that the way it worked when you get fired or laid off is that you're also suddenly on the hook to pay back the last, say, 6 months of your income.

I can't recall a situation like this, with an ecosystem of third-party clients collecting subscriptions and then having the first-party service yank the carpet out from under them — and their customers — with zero warning or sunset period.
Alas, this situation has become a recurring theme.

It's clear now that ultimately wasn't their intent. If they wanted something that could work for everyone, they would have simply made an effort to listen, instead of being dishonest, callous, and punitive in pricing.
You seriously have no idea how easy product development is when your north star is just 'listen to what people are saying'.
I find Selig's good spirits in the face of this debacle to be inspiring.
key_insight: The recurring pattern of platforms killing third-party clients without warning creates a uniquely cruel financial trap where developers owe refunds on revenue already earned, turning a lost job into an active debt.
